name: pestle style pesto pasta
notes:
The mortar/pestle version of pesto pasta. Store-bought basil pesto isn't good enough here — the mortar pesto has texture and freshness that a jarred product can't match.

GREENS (soft leaves only): use basil, baby arugula (mixed greens), or spinach. All three pestle well into a paste. Parsley does NOT work — it's too fibrous and doesn't break down under the pestle. The greens go into the mortar with the rest of the ingredients.

NUTS / SEEDS (soft only): pine nuts are canonical. Cashews and sunflower seeds also work. ALMONDS DON'T WORK — they're too hard to break down by hand; you need a blender or food processor for almonds (which is a different recipe entirely).

GARLIC HANDLING: garlic in a pestle pesto is potent. Two ways to manage it:
- Use sparingly. A single clove is usually enough for one batch.
- Or pre-macerate in lemon juice for a few minutes, then discard most of the lemon juice before pestling the garlic in. The maceration softens the raw garlic bite, but it's easy to make the pesto too lemony if you leave too much juice — drain aggressively.

CREAM IS AN OPTIONAL FINISH: stir in a splash of heavy whipping cream at the end for a richer, more silken pesto. Not traditional but works.

TOMATOES (OPTIONAL, FRESH ONLY): fresh tomatoes finely chopped and rinsed can ride alongside as a secondary addition — adds brightness and structure. No canned tomatoes (wrong texture for a mortar dish).

RAISINS (OPTIONAL): a small handful of raisins works as a sicilian-leaning sweet-savory move. Good with the pine-nut and basil base.
